# Transcrição: 12- Endings

- **Fonte:** 12- Endings
- **Duração:** 06:58
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:00]

Now it's time to talk about endings. I know we have stuff to cover in the middle, but as I said, beginnings and endings are the most important parts of stories. They're in conversation with each other, so it only makes sense that we cover those first. Let's talk about endings. The first thing you have to think about in terms of endings is understanding what you're trying to say and then show it before you say it. Does that make sense to you? What we want to do in a story is to let the audience experience the moment that we had and then we can reiterate it in the words we choose. In my blood draw story, the moment that I experienced was the man drawing my blood, the best blood draw I've ever had, I cry in my car. That is showing you how I felt. Then I explained it to you by talking about my daughter and her peanut allergy and how I don't dismiss her fears anymore. At the ending of a story, you want to show it and then tell it. The mistake people make is they often just tell the ending. They say what they thought. They say how they felt. They say how they changed over time, but that's not how a story should be. The movie should continue to play in the audience's mind. They should be able to envision the change taking place and then if you want to reiterate it in words, you can reiterate it any way that you want, but we show it and then we say it. We also always end in the heart of the storyteller. You always end with yourself. If you're experiencing a moment of transformation or realization and there's another person involved, the story should not end on the other person. It should not end with the look they gave you or the words they said to you. The story always must come back here. It must always be about you and it must reside in your heart and mind at the end. Keep it internal. If it's a professional story, it's the same thing. You're going to keep that story internal, whether it is internal to the product that you're trying to sell, the service you're offering, the story of your company, the story of your ascent up the corporate ladder, whatever it is, it must ultimately land here inside. You'll also want to consider what not to put in the end of your story because creating a sense of wonder can be extremely powerful too. What is the audience going to continue to wonder about after the story is finished? I like to think of stories as coats. When we tell a story, what we're doing is we're putting a coat on our audience and the sooner they can get the coat off, the sooner they can forget your story. But if you give them something to continue to wonder about, if you don't fully complete

## [03:00 - 06:02]

the story, if you don't tie up all the loose ends, oftentimes it's hard to get that coat off. There's probably television shows and movies that you've watched where the ending wasn't complete. There was still some stuff that you were wondering about and even though the movie or the book or the show is now done, it still resides in your mind. It still stays in your heart. You can't stop thinking about it because there were unanswered questions. Wonderful powerful stories often lead to unanswered questions. In my bad blood draw story, you may be wondering, was every blood draw after that one better because of the encounter I had with the man? I chose not to tell you. I gave you a singular day in my life and I did not spool it ahead. I didn't tell you if that changed the nature of all needles for me for the rest of my life. I wanted you to wonder. I wanted you to wonder what was going on with me in the future so that the next time you go to get a blood draw or a flu shot, I want you to think about me. I want you to be wondering what it's like for me today. That creates enormous power in a story. That creates gravity that holds your audience down with you. You want to consider what you want to say and what you want to leave out. Then, if you're speaking your story, if you're speaking it to an audience, you want to think about how you're going to say the end of your story. Pacing is critical at the end of a story. One of the tricky things about telling a story, whether it's your marketing plan or a story you're standing on stage and speaking or an inspirational address, is it doesn't have a timer affixed to it. An audience doesn't know how close they are to an end of a story. There's no way of them knowing. In a movie, you know the running time. You can pause the movie at home and check how much time is left. But you have a sense of about how long a movie is going to be. But with a story, it could be three minutes. It could be six minutes. It could be 22 minutes. That's an enormous variance. This is like to know when they're getting close to the end. As you're getting close to the end, you want to signal to the audience that I'm about to say the most important thing I'm going to say. I'm reaching the climax. I'm reaching the reason I opened my mouth in the first place. One of the best ways to signal that is through pacing, through the slowing of your pace, through the quieting of your voice. These are signals that audiences understand. They signal this is important. You're getting close to the end. Lean in. I'm about to say the thing I've been dying to say. I'm about to tell you the purpose of all the words that preceded it. This is the reason I told the story. So think about those things at the end of your story. Make sure to show it, then say it.

## [06:02 - 06:58]

Make sure that you end with yourself, in your heart, in your mind. Make a decision about where you're going to end. What are you going to leave off? How much wonder are you going to create at the end of your story? And then how are you going to tell it so it has the maximum impact? That is how you end a story. That is how you fulfill that promise you made at the beginning of your story. We start great signaling to the audience that they're on a good ride, that we're going to lead them to something good. And the ending is the fulfillment of that promise, and that is how you fulfill it. End your story well. The beginning of the story is a promise that time is going to be well spent, that we're going to say something of import and meaning. And the ending is the fulfillment of that promise. Land the ending well, and your audience will love you for it.
